subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree from a U.S. university, majoring in Law or Pre-Law.
Participated in moot court and legal research seminars during undergraduate studies.
Completed coursework in legal writing, constitutional law, and technology law.
Received additional training in AI regulation as part of academic focus.
Served as a research assistant to law professors during studies.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Judicial Law Clerk supporting legal research and case analysis.
Works as a research assistant for law professors, focusing on legal research and academic writing.
Contributes to academic journals through legal essays and case commentaries.
Participates in moot court competitions, preparing briefs and oral arguments.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the legal sector, with a focus on academia and judicial research.
Specializes in technology law and AI regulation within the legal field.
Engages regularly with regulatory and compliance aspects of artificial intelligence.
Operates at the intersection of law and technology.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed within a university law faculty setting.
Works closely with small academic teams and other clerks.
Collaborates with professors, journal editors, and moot court teams.
Environment is research-focused, with access to legal databases and advanced tech tools.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Academic legal research and higher education segment.
Supports both educational objectives and scholarly legal publications.
Involved in legal technology and regulatory research projects.
Market segment includes legal academia, judicial support, and technology law.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has less than 1 year of experience as a judicial law clerk and research assistant.
Started current law clerk and research assistant roles immediately after undergraduate graduation.
Experience includes both academic and court-related legal research.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level professional, early in legal career.
Holds junior or early-career research positions within academia.
Acts as a supporting member of legal research and moot court teams.
Regularly receives mentorship and supervision from law professors.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Performs extensive legal research using LexisNexis, Westlaw, and online databases.
Drafts academic essays, journal articles, and legal memos.
Prepares case briefs and analyses for moot court and classroom discussions.
Supports law professors with research for publications and class material.
Uses AI tools to summarize and analyze lengthy legal documents.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of work hours on legal research and case reading.
Dedicates 30% of time to writing academic and legal materials.
Allocates 20% to AI-assisted document analysis and tool integration.
Reserves around 10% for meetings, moot court preparation, and administrative tasks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates under guidance of law professors and journal editors.
Has autonomy in selecting research strategies and initial case sources.
Responsible for synthesizing findings and drafting initial analyses.
Major decisions (e.g., final arguments, publication content) require supervisor approval.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Summarizing case facts and legal holdings.
Using AI tools to generate initial drafts or summaries of legal texts.
Searching for precedents in LexisNexis or Westlaw using established queries.
Writing case briefs and preparing outlines for class discussions.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Analyzing and synthesizing conflicting legal precedents.
Drafting original legal arguments for complex, novel issues.
Navigating ambiguous or poorly-structured legal databases.
Ensuring precision and accuracy in AI-assisted legal research outputs.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Bulk reading and summarization of hundreds of pages of court cases.
Extracting statutory references or case citations from lengthy documents.
Compiling bibliographies for academic legal papers.
Transcribing or organizing legal notes and case facts.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys formulating novel legal arguments and engaging in moot court advocacy.
Finds satisfaction in deep-diving into regulatory and compliance research on AI.
Considers repetitive data extraction and citation formatting tedious.
Prefers interactive, analytical work over rote summarization or manual document parsing.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Managing extremely long case documents with limited search/filter capabilities.
Manually verifying the accuracy of AI-generated summaries or citations.
Integrating AI tools with legacy legal databases (e.g., Westlaw export issues).
Switching between multiple platforms increases friction and time spent.

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Productivity is highest when using AI tools to automate repetitive research tasks.
Enjoyment increases with opportunities for creative legal analysis and advocacy.
Skill development is fastest in areas where AI tools supplement, not replace, work.
Tedious, manual tasks tend to reduce engagement and perceived productivity.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Highly proactive in exploring emerging AI and legal tech solutions.
Views technology adoption as essential for efficiency in legal research.
Enjoys experimenting with new legal AI tools in workflow.
Feels comfortable being an early adopter in the academic legal environment.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new digital research platforms and AI tools.
Has successfully integrated Google AI Studio and Notebook LM into daily tasks.
Able to troubleshoot and locally deploy open-source AI models as needed.
Finds minimal friction when switching between different legal tech tools.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to invest time in learning features of advanced AI platforms.
Has self-trained on Google AI Studio, Notebook LM, and legal database integrations.
Regularly keeps up-to-date with latest developments in legal AI tools.
Views tech training as beneficial for both current role and future career growth.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by desire to improve efficiency and reduce manual labor.
Learning is prioritized if tools directly enhance legal research or writing.
Heavy workload during academic peaks can limit available time for new tech exploration.
Occasional frustration with lack of integration between legal and AI platforms.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers in academic setting often share recommendations on effective AI tools.
Law professors encourage adoption of technology for research and compliance tasks.
Collaborative environment supports knowledge sharing on tool usage.
Positive peer feedback encourages continued experimentation with new legal tech.

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Google AI Studio for long-context document analysis and summarization.
Relies on Notebook LM by Google for audio-based case review and artificial 'podcast' summaries.
Accesses LexisNexis and Westlaw daily for legal research and precedent search.
Utilizes cloud-based storage for organizing legal documents and notes.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Uses Microsoft Word and Google Docs for drafting legal memos and essays.
Manages references and citations with Zotero or similar bibliography tools.
Employs PDF annotation tools (like Adobe Acrobat) for case highlighting.
Occasionally uses Excel or Google Sheets for tracking research progress.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Highly proficient with LexisNexis and Westlaw advanced search features.
Familiar with Google AI Studio's prompt engineering and context management.
Has working knowledge of Notebook LM and its conversational summarization functions.
Understands basic integration between legal research platforms and cloud storage.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Experiences some friction exporting data from Westlaw/LexisNexis to AI platforms.
Manual transfer of case files between legal databases and AI tools is occasionally required.
Integration between reference managers and AI tools is limited or non-existent.
Switching between multiple platforms can disrupt workflow continuity.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands core concepts like large language models, tokenization, and hallucination.
Familiar with terms such as prompt engineering, context window, and model fine-tuning.
Has basic knowledge of machine learning risks, including bias and data privacy.
Can explain the difference between open-source and proprietary AI models.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses Google AI Studio daily for analyzing lengthy legal texts.
Employs Notebook LM by Google for summarizing batches of case readings.
Has experimented with local deployment of open-source language models.
Rarely uses ChatGPT; prefers alternatives with longer context windows.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI tools every day to process, summarize, and analyze legal documents.
Applies AI for note-taking, extracting relevant legal issues, and drafting briefs.
Relies on AI for quickly reviewing large volumes of assigned readings.
Uses AI to create artificial 'podcasts' for passive review of case content.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI tools have significantly reduced time spent on summarizing hundreds of pages.
Occasionally, AI-generated summaries miss subtle legal nuances or misstate holdings.
Learned to always verify AI outputs before including them in academic writing.
Success with Google AI Studio is attributed to its long-context handling and low hallucination rate.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal organizational training, but peer-to-peer learning is common.
Regularly exchanges tips and best practices with other law students and researchers.
Access to online legal tech forums and university-sponsored webinars.
Relies on self-guided exploration and community documentation for AI tools.

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools for complex legal analysis.
Uses AI independently for simple tasks like summarization or extraction.
Combines human review with AI output to ensure accuracy and compliance.
Adapts interaction mode based on the complexity of the legal issue at hand.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for drafting and research tasks.
Occasionally treats AI as a co-worker, especially for brainstorming arguments.
Uses AI as an advisor when seeking alternative perspectives on legal reasoning.
Maintains final editorial control over all AI-generated content.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to experimenting with new AI-driven workflows.
Adopts AI solutions that demonstrably improve efficiency or accuracy.
Regularly integrates new AI features as they become available.
Advocates for increased AI use in legal education and research.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational AI interfaces for complex reasoning and iterative tasks.
Uses command-based prompts for quick, targeted information extraction.
Finds conversational interfaces more intuitive for legal analysis discussions.
Switches between interfaces based on task urgency and depth required.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Legal research and advocacy require deep understanding of jurisdiction-specific precedents.
Case analysis often depends on nuanced interpretation of legal reasoning.
AI tools can assist, but not fully replace, tacit knowledge in legal argumentation.
Regularly supplements AI outputs with personal expertise and professor guidance.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Moderate trust in AI for initial research and summarization tasks.
Low trust in AI for final drafting of legal arguments or academic submissions.
Always performs manual review and verification before relying on AI-generated content.
Trust increases with platforms (like Google AI Studio) known for low hallucination rates.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers high AI automation for repetitive or data-heavy tasks (e.g., summarization).
Maintains human involvement for tasks requiring critical analysis or creativity.
Ideal workflow is AI-assisted but always human-edited for legal work.
Sees AI as a tool to streamline, not replace, core responsibilities.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for administrative and citation-related tasks.
Views high automation as risky for tasks involving legal judgment or advocacy.
Threat perception increases if AI bypasses mandatory human review steps.
Supports automation as long as override and audit mechanisms are present.

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: AI has enhanced the importance of critical thinking and legal synthesis skills.
Routine research and summarization skills are less emphasized due to automation.
Effective use of AI requires adapting traditional legal research techniques.
Emphasizes the need to develop skills in prompt engineering and AI oversight.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Prompt engineering and crafting effective AI queries are increasingly important.
Oversight and verification of AI-generated legal content is a new responsibility.
Understanding AI risk, bias, and compliance in legal settings is now essential.
Exposure to open-source AI deployment and legal tech integration has expanded skill set.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern about automation of repetitive research and citation tasks.
Believes core legal analysis and advocacy roles are less susceptible to AI replacement.
Monitors industry trends for potential shifts in job responsibilities due to AI.
Views AI as a complement rather than a threat to overall career trajectory.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review of all AI outputs is a standard part of workflow.
No critical legal documents are submitted without human verification.
Maintains version control and retains original documents for cross-checking.
Seeks AI tools that provide transparency and audit logs for all outputs.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Academic environment is increasingly supportive of AI tool experimentation.
No formal AI policies yet, but best practices are evolving through peer discussions.
Professors encourage responsible AI use with proper oversight.
Anticipates future institutional guidelines as AI adoption grows.

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will fundamentally reshape legal research and compliance.
Sees potential for improved access to justice via AI-driven legal tools.
Recognizes risks related to bias, misinformation, and over-automation.
Values AI's ability to increase legal research efficiency and accessibility.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Prioritizes accuracy, transparency, and accountability in AI applications.
Concerned about potential for AI-generated errors in legal documents.
Supports robust oversight and human-in-the-loop approaches for legal AI.
Advocates for responsible AI deployment, especially in high-stakes contexts.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires seamless integration between AI platforms and legacy legal databases.
Wishes for AI tools with built-in citation verification and error detection.
Needs more advanced, context-aware summarization for complex legal cases.
Would benefit from AI models trained specifically on jurisdictional law.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates increased reliance on AI for initial legal research and document review.
Expects human roles to shift further toward oversight, quality control, and strategic analysis.
Foresees emergence of new legal tech specialties and interdisciplinary roles.
Believes AI will drive greater demand for tech-savvy legal professionals.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Develop formal guidelines for responsible AI use in academic legal research.
Offer structured training on AI tool integration and risk management.
Encourage interdisciplinary collaboration between law and computer science departments.
Invest in AI tools tailored for legal research and compliance.
